holding: Administrative area inspections of private premises generally require a warrant, but probable cause for an administrative inspection warrant is judged by reasonable legislative/administrative standards (e.g., area-wide code enforcement) - not individualized criminal probable cause.
point_cited_for: Administrative/area inspection warrant; relaxed administrative probable cause (already on page - confirmed)

cite: See v. City of Seattle, 387 U.S. 541 (1967)
holding: Camara's administrative-warrant rule extends to commercial premises: a business owner may insist on a warrant before an administrative inspection of non-public areas.
point_cited_for: Administrative-inspection warrant requirement extended to commercial premises

holding: OSHA could not conduct warrantless inspections of business premises; an administrative inspection generally requires a warrant. Recognized the closely-regulated-industry exception as a narrow carve-out.
point_cited_for: Administrative inspections need a warrant; defines the closely-regulated-industry exception

holding: Warrantless inspection of a closely regulated business (auto junkyard/vehicle dismantler) is reasonable if: (1) a substantial government interest informs the regulatory scheme, (2) warrantless inspection is necessary to further it, and (3) the scheme provides a constitutionally adequate substitute for a warrant (notice + limits on inspector discretion).
point_cited_for: Three-part test for warrantless inspection of closely-regulated industries

holding: School searches of students require neither a warrant nor probable cause - only reasonableness at inception and in scope. Justice Blackmun's concurrence articulated the 'special needs, beyond the normal need for law enforcement' formulation that names the doctrine.
point_cited_for: Origin of the 'special needs' label (Blackmun concurrence); reasonableness standard for school searches

case: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n
cite: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n, 489 U.S. 602 (1989)
holding: Special-needs doctrine: warrantless, suspicionless drug/alcohol testing of railroad employees after accidents is reasonable; the government's special need (railroad safety) beyond ordinary law enforcement justifies dispensing with warrant and individualized suspicion after a balancing of interests.
point_cited_for: Special-needs balancing; suspicionless testing upheld for safety-sensitive employees

case: National Treasury Employees Union v. Von Raab
cite: National Treasury Employees Union v. Von Raab, 489 U.S. 656 (1989)
holding: Companion to Skinner: suspicionless drug testing of Customs employees seeking promotion to drug-interdiction/firearm-carrying positions is a reasonable special-needs search; warrant and individualized suspicion not required.
point_cited_for: Special-needs suspicionless testing of government employees in safety/integrity-sensitive roles

holding: A probationer's home may be searched on 'reasonable grounds' (less than probable cause) under a special needs rationale - the operation of a probation system presents special needs beyond normal law enforcement that justify departure from the warrant/probable-cause requirements.
point_cited_for: Special-needs rationale applied to probation/community supervision
